title: Keynote - Era of experience (Prof. David Silver)
text:
Hi everyone, it's a a great pleasure to
be here. Um, thanks for the invitation.
Um, so today I am going to talk about
the era of experience.
So if we look at the state of AI today,
I would say that um the majority of the
work which has been done it is best
characterized by something called the
era of human data.
The way that the AI has been trained is
primarily learning from human data. It's
trained from the internet. There's lots
of language that's been found on the
internet. You know, we have these
amazing LLMs that are trained on every
piece of human data that can be found on
the internet. And then they're
fine-tuned from further human data, from
human feedback, from human preferences,
human examples. Um, so that our systems
are primarily built from human data. And
this has led to enormous progress. We
have LLMs with wide ranging knowledge,
incredible capabilities like all the the
progress that's happened. You know,
really I I think many people would point
to LLMs as being um you know, the reason
that that's happened. Um we have amazing
creations that can solve law problems or
math problems or software problems.
However, there is a a major butt with
these systems and I think it's basically
comes down to the fact that we're not
done. that human data, the era of human
data in itself leads to AI systems that
can't in isolation go all the way to
super intelligence.
Why is that? Well, it's because they
can't discover new knowledge. These are
systems which have been built from human
knowledge. We haven't put into them the
capability to discover knowledge for
themselves.
So, they're basically stuck in the
paradigms that humans have built.
They're not yet ready to create new
paradigms. And I think furthermore, it's
fair to say that we haven't yet answered
the deepest scientific question for the
whole field of AI, which is how can an
agent learn for itself.
So I want to give as an example um you
know a little bit of contrast uh this
video. Um it's a sped up video of a a
very um uh typical human baby u which is
exploring its environment and it's just
interacting. It's interacting and
playing. It's kind of moving around this
playroom and just playing endlessly with
all of these objects around itself.
There's no one, you know, at this point.
I mean, it's I'm not arguing that humans
uh babies never receive verbal
instructions or or learn from uh other
humans, but at least in this example, I
think it's very characteristic of a
major way in which humans learn, which
is through experience. Like this baby is
like just playing. It's setting itself
sub goals. It's picking, you know, a new
toy to play with, experimenting with
with it, figuring out every single way
that that toy can be manipulated. Um,
and then it moves on to the next toy,
tries playing with it. Occasionally, it
discovers something interesting in the
room, like a little kind of boingy thing
on the on the on the wall that keeps
stops the door from closing, and it will
just play with that for a while. Um, you
know, it's just fascinated by the world
around the world around it and interacts
continually to learn more and more about
how the world works. And this same
mechanism allows this baby to one day
develop these skills in such a way that
they can be put to many many different
uses. And so this baby might grow up to
be a neurosurgeon, a dancer, a
craftsman, a pianist, a scientist, or a
tennis player. Um, but all of those
skills are developed in this way, you
know, through interaction.
So I think it's fair to say that an
inevitable transition is starting
towards something which I call the era
of experience.
Um, and that basically means that agents
will learn primarily from experience.
They'll learn through interacting with
their environment at vast scales. And
they will interact again and again and
again, learning more and more about how
the world works. And I think this will
one day happen at scales that vastly
exceed the scale of the internet. Now
the internet seems so large to us the
amount of knowledge that humans have
developed but one day this will seem
tiny compared to the knowledge that
agents have acquired for themselves
and this transition will transform AI.
There will be continual discovery of new
knowledge and new capabilities and this
ultimately will lead to superhuman
intelligence.
So we can think of some of the
characteristics that differentiate that
baby in the video from the current era
that we're in. And I think just a few of
those characteristics would be that you
know the agents in era of experience
will inhabit streams of experience.
Doesn't have to be the same stream as
that baby. It could be you know
artificial streams uh streams of
whatever the agent needs to interact
with in its environment. Um but its
actions and observations will be richly
grounded in the environment. They won't
just be kind of um dialogue. There'll be
things that the agent can actually do
that transform its world and it will
receive rich observations of that world
in return. And the rewards that that
agency receives its goals its goals will
be defined in and grounded in the
experience of the world. Not just you
know like someone some writer saying
that that action was good or bad. Yeah.
will actually do something in the world
and and that thing has a consequence on
the world which will be good or which
will be bad and the agent will learn
from that consequence and the agents
will plan um or reason about the
experience. They'll reason about the
actual interactions it's h having in the
world um not at some abstract way
removed from its experience in the
world.
So then we could ask you know why um why
hasn't this happened before? you know,
weren't weren't we here a few years ago?
Um, and I think it was true that there
was a lot of excitement um in particular
in reinforcement learning, which is um a
paradigm focused on learning from
experience and that led to some great
successes in simulated environments with
clear rewards. Uh things like Atari,
Alph Go, Alpha Zero, you know, these
were some successes that that that we
had and there were many more from other
places as well. Um but I think what's
happened is you know the era of human
data has come along and in some sense um
although that brought many many
successes in a way we threw out the baby
with the bathwater we stopped focusing
on how to learn how to keep adapting how
to keep acquiring knowledge from the
environment um and so I think you know
there's a turning point that has
happened as we start to transition
towards the era of experience um and the
systems which are starting to get put
into um into motion Today I think at the
beginning of this transition to the era
of experience I should clarify the
y-axis on this plot is not you know how
good these things are. I'm not arguing
that LLMs are inherently bad and that
you know reinforcement learning is
inherently good. This is really about
where the attention of the field is
focused. And I think that in order to
achieve superhuman intelligence, I think
it's unarguable that we will need to
transition to a greater attention um
that's primarily focused on learning
from experience.
So I think one way we could characterize
this is you know the field has been
looking under a a lampost. Um and most
people are looking under that same
lampost. uh you know the whole field of
AI has converged collapsed on one
approach which is you know the LLM
approach to to AI um and that's brought
lots of good things but it's not the
only part of AI to be explored um you
know there's there's another lampost
that that doesn't have a lot of
attention um and that's the lampost
which is represented here by the year of
experience and when we start to explore
under that lampost we will discover
great progress
so you know why did this happen? Well, I
think you know it happened for very
natural reasons. Like I think human data
u provided the shortcut. It allowed us
to solve what I call the shallow problem
of AI. Like this the shallow problem is
basically you know how can you distill
all the knowledge that's already out
there into an agent. And that's the
shallow problem of AI. We've done a
really great job of that. We've taken
all of the knowledge that's found on the
internet and we've put it into our
agents. So you can think of this as like
you know to borrow an analogy we can
think of this as um you know we
discovered fossil fuels um on the
internet like you know the coal um is
the the all of the human data that's
already exists on the internet um and
that by you know exploiting those fossil
fuels um we were able to kind of you
know make enormous progress very very
quickly and that allowed us to you know
cover all of the things that's that's in
that data. um you know it has enormous
generality and it's far cheaper like
fossil fuels it's very cheap to to to
burn that data um and it's cheaper than
interacting with the world um but the
problem is that we've used up most of
the fuel so if you think about it
pre-training you know I'm not saying
there'll be no more gains in
pre-training but a lot of the easy gains
have been taken um and that paradigm is
certainly slowing down um so how can we
go beyond that well we need experience
to go beyond that so you can think of
this in the analogy as renewable energy
something that will allow us to keep
learning forever. Like there is no limit
to the amount of interaction that an
agent can do with its environment. Um
and then how can you get that same kind
of generality? You know, how can we get
beyond um the the initial like um
simulated examples I saw before and have
very very general systems that learn
from experience. Well, I think the
answer there is in the world. You know,
the world is extremely rich. Like that
baby's environment has so many signals
in it for it to kind of choose to
optimize. you know, one moment it can
pick up this toy and try and turn it
over. Another moment it can try and you
know, manipulate its own body and move
around in a particular way or or you
know, move the door or something. There
are vast numbers of signals in the world
to optimize. Um, and that will enable
huge generality in systems that learn
everything there is to learn to to
master their own environment.
But, you know, we have to pay the cost
of interaction. you know, the shortcuts
are going to run out and we have to
choose to actually run agents that
interact with their environment. We have
to pay that cost to to to learn. Um, and
we have to allow our systems to make
mistakes so that we can actually build
things that learn from those mistakes
and and get better and learn to avoid
making worse mistakes later.
Okay. So, if we look back then, you
know, you can ask, well, does this
paradigm have the potential to succeed?
And I think we can see from the past
examples that there has been an enormous
amount of success that when people have
pushed on this approach, it has yielded
great successes. You know, it's it's
something that when we have placed our
attention under that lampost, it has
worked. Um, so in board games, you know,
we've built superhuman systems for chess
and go and many other board games. Um,
in video games, we've built superhuman
systems at Atari and Starcraft and many
other video games. you know, we've um
built incredible robotic systems that
can manipulate and locomote in in in
very clever ways. Um and in mathematics,
I'll talk a little bit about this, but
you know, we've achieved IMO medals and
um beat human um ability at doing, you
know, matrix operations.
Every time we've pushed on these
systems, it has yielded success. Um and
so, you know, I think it's just about
where do we choose to put our attention?
So I'm going to talk a little bit about
some of those case case studies just to
talk a little bit about um you know what
does it look like to make progress
towards the era of experience and I'm
going to start with something which you
know is many years ago something
probably you know many of you all know
about um but I think is like a good
example to remind us of the capabilities
that these systems have um so this was
something we did in um uh 2017 2018 um
and we built a system called Alpha Zero
um which was a successor to Alph Go, but
it was a very beautiful system and it
was beautiful because it it actually
learned from scratch like it just
started from the random weights in a
neural network um with no human
knowledge at all. So in some sense it's
the extreme example of the the other
lampost. Uh and I'm not saying every
system has to start from scratch but
just you know in some sense it's the
most beautiful example if you can do
that. It really illustrates the point
very well. And so alpha zero you know um
it was a very simple algorithm um it ran
a particular tree search called Monte
Carlo tree search that made use of two
neural networks one to represent its
policy how it takes actions and another
um that estimated how well it was doing
the value function and so that was its
planning process. It just you know did a
tree search informed by those neural
networks. The second step was it updated
the policy towards the best action that
was found by its um by its search by its
tree search. And the third step was it
updated the value function towards the
outcome of these self-play games. So
that's um policy evaluation. And so
these three steps they're as simple as
they are here. There were no like hidden
tricks, you know, to it was just very
straightforward algorithm. And when you
unleash this algorithm, it starts from
random weights and it ends up in a few
hours um defeating the best um
handcrafted programs and becoming
superhuman and actually going off to
beat the the world champions in in many
different games, chess, shogi, go um
other games as well.
Um so you know here are some of the
learning curves where you see that um in
just a few hours it was able to um
exceed the world champion in each of
chess shogi and go um the world champion
um program which was already superhuman
in in these cases.
Um and so you know in this case it
worked. Um I want to talk about two more
recent um um uh pieces of work now. Um
actually it's very timely because both
of them are being um coming out uh in
nature. one um just came out last week
and one's coming out next week I think.
Um so alpha proof um is something we did
last year but it's um we're we're
finally publishing the the paper. Um and
the idea is is is very again you know as
as simple as the the the game here. We
we treat mathematics as a game like what
happens if we treat the whole of
mathematics as like a complex game and
try and solve that game with exactly
those same simple steps that were
described here. [snorts] Um and so how
can you treat mathematics as a game?
Well, luckily people have formalized
this game already for us. Um so we used
um a formalization of mathematics called
leen u which is like a programming
language for mathematics which allows um
all mathematics that can be expressed um
is is possible to express in lean. So
it's very general um and lean basically
allows you to um you know write down
theorems um in a particular um way as a
piece of code and then you apply tactics
like actions to try and you know
transform the state of this this prover
until you end up in a in a proven state
and at that point you know that you have
formally solved the problem like it's
done you know you've you've formally
proved that that this theorem is is um
is is correct or you failed to prove it
[snorts] um and And that gives a reward
signal. And we use that reward signal to
train a system that learns via this
alphazero approach to solve the game of
mathematics.
[snorts] And when you look at the
learning curves, you get this beautiful
progress just like we saw in in in the
games of of chess and go. So on the
left, we see what happens when we learn
from experience. Can I ask that no one
shares this photo yet because it's
coming out like next week. So I put it
up, but don't don't share this yet.
Um, [snorts] so I saw some u yeah some
cameras coming out. Um, so just wait for
it to come out and then you can. Um, so
so the the learning from experience um
is is what you see on the left. Um and
this is basically an amazing smooth
progression of just learning from a a
minimal piece of knowledge like it's not
quite starting from scratch but it
starts from you know very minimal amount
of knowledge and the solving process
here does not use any human language
like you know once it once the problems
have been formalized and it's knows what
it's trying to do like it just we give
it like you know millions of these
different problems um in in lean and
then the solving process is is something
that's discovered you know, just by
in the same way that that Alph Go
learned to solve Go. Um, [snorts] and it
it ends up with this very strong
performance. Um, on the right, we see
what happens when we plan from
experience. And that basically means
that we apply exactly the same algorithm
but to the particular problem that we're
starting with. So rather than starting
with all, you know, a huge set of
problems, we now just focus on on one
problem and say, look, if I try really
hard to solve this problem, um, how how
much progress do I make on that? Um, so
that's kind of a planning from
experience. Um, and that just improves
even more and you end up with this
incredible performance. Um, and this
system last year became the first ever
system to achieve a medal in the
International Mathematics Olympiad. Um,
it was just one point short of a gold
medal in fact. Um, and this year there
have been um, you know, multiple systems
that have achieved a gold medal um,
using you know LLMs and so forth. So I
don't want to claim this was the only
approach that can can succeed in
mathematics but I do think it's an
example again where if we push we will
see success.
Um here is a very beautiful proof that
it came up with. So, this was a problem
that was solved by the International
Mathematics Olympiad, for those who
don't know, is like ridiculously hard
mathematics problems that are just so
hard that that like the best young
mathematicians in the world like
basically go into boot camps where they
train for these problems um for like a
year straight and do nothing else apart
from training for for these problems and
they're they're you know become field
medalists and they're some of the best
mathematicians in the world. Um [snorts]
so less than 2% of the mathematicians in
the competition solved this problem. Um
but alpha proof solved it. Um and this
is the solution it came up with you know
in lean. This is the piece of code it it
discovered through this kind of alpha
zero process of like you know planning
through experience um on top of its
weights.
Okay. Finally I want to talk about
another you know uh piece of work that
again is just about to come out and
again we actually did this a couple of
years back but we're able to publish
this now. [snorts] Um so this is uh some
work I find very beautiful on on
discovering algorithms. So I think you
know if you ask you you know are we done
with reinforcement learning? I think the
answer is no. Um RL is very hard. Um you
know why is it hard? I think it's hard
because unlike you know supervised
learning it has a non-ifferiable
objective. the best recipe varies
between environments and there's no
general purpose kind of um single
general purpose algorithm that just
works across all the different problems
you might want to apply RL to. So in
that sense, you know, we're not done.
There's still work to do. [snorts] Um
but one thing we can do which works
remarkably well is to actually apply
this same idea at the meta level. So we
actually learned some RL algorithms from
experience. So, what does that look
like? It basically means that we created
an agent um that could play um all kinds
of different games, could be put into
different simulated environments, you
know, we try kind of put it into lots of
different environments. And we tried we
we kind of built a neural network that
represented the algorithm that actually
uses to learn. [snorts] And then we
learned by trial and error which of
those algorithms is actually most
effective across those different um
instances of the agent trying to solve
different problems. So it's basically
you know you can imagine that rather
than starting with someone saying you
know here your algorithm is Q-learning
or your algorithm is TD learning or your
algorithm is policy gradient it doesn't
start with any of those and it has a
neural network that can in principle
express any of them and it kind of has
to figure out what the best algorithm
is. Um and so it's kind of try trying to
to to learn and while it's learning it
kind of learns better and better how to
learn. [snorts] Um and if we look at the
progress that it makes um it actually
ends up beating the u best ever created
reinforcement learning algorithms you
know these are algorithms that we
ourselves spent years creating. So
things like you know the u so there's
m0ero um is the the best baseline for
example on Atari that's um never
previously been defeated. Um, and this
discovered algorithm handily defeats it.
Um, and what's more, it works on on
environments that it's never seen
before. So, it actually generalizes to
environments that it was not trained
upon. So, it learns discovers an
algorithm that's so general that it can
be applied to things it's never seen
before. So, here we found something that
actually discovers a reinforcement
learning algorithm that's better than
the best human created algorithm, you
know, and there's been a lot of hard
labor going into creating these things.
Um, and one of the really nice things
about this is that what we see is that
as we start to expose the agent to more
and more training environments,
um, it starts to perform better and
better and better across, um, um, all of
these different environments and indeed
across held out environments. So you can
think of this as a new scaling law for
AI that as we start to expose our agents
to more sources of experience, the agent
gets better at learning from that
experience. It's basically exposed to
just more and more diverse streams of
experience and then it figures out oh
you know this is the best way to learn
across those and and and it learns
something very general and very robust.
So for the first time we have a recipe
for robust and general reinforcement
learning and I see this as something
which will help us you know to really um
you know see what is underneath that
second lampost.
Okay. So um those are the case studies.
Um so I really just wanted to finish
with a with a call to arms. So I think
you know if nothing else I've been
trying to persuade you today um just to
challenge challenge the status quo of
how people are thinking about AI um and
the real call to arms I think is to try
and solve the deep problem of AI you
know the deep problem of AI how to learn
from experience
and like that baby invidia you know
something which is just able to keep
learning and adapting and figuring
things out um and acquiring knowledge
for itself and when we achieve that this
will be a profound moment for the whole
science of AI and it will transform the
future of AI and I believe through that
the future of humanity.
Thank you.
